our_argument_summary: Section 5.15 argues that the dome's GitHub repository IS the complete computational model, not a simplified public summary. It supports this with four examples: (1) Schumann resonance uses uniform cavity height f=c/4H₀ rather than integrating over H(r), (2) curve-fitting scripts fit to WGS84 coordinates rather than deriving from physics, (3) update_optical_caveats.py suppresses the sun/firmament collision contradiction with labels rather than resolving it, (4) inject_ai_layer.py hardcodes core parameters as static values. The closing argument: if a more complete model exists, it is not in the repository, not in the source code, and not reproducible.

holes_found.description: DOCX format entirely omits this section. As the SEC-5.14 review noted, Sections 5.14-5.16 are present in generate-html.js but absent from build-doc-v4.js. This means the Word document and PDF versions of the review — the formats most likely to be shared, cited, or printed — completely lack one of the review's most original philosophical arguments. A dome defender who only reads the DOCX version would never encounter this section. This is especially problematic because SEC-5.15 is the section that preemptively closes the 'you're only critiquing the simplified version' defense — without it, that defense remains open.
holes_found.recommendation: Add Sections 5.14, 5.15, and 5.16 to build-doc-v4.js as a priority. This is the third curmudgeon review flagging this gap.

holes_found.description: The Schumann formula claim is subtly imprecise. Section 5.15 says the dome computes Schumann resonance using 'f = c/4H₀' and that 'no script in the repository averages H(r) over the dome's exponential profile.' But the dome's own model page (05-model.txt line 63-64) presents the formula as H = c/(4×f_Schumann) — i.e., it solves for H FROM the known Schumann frequency, not predicting f FROM H. This is an important distinction: the dome model uses the Schumann resonance to calibrate its cavity height, not to predict the resonance from dome geometry. A smart dome defender would say: 'Of course we don't compute Schumann from H₀ — we derived H₀ from Schumann. The formula runs in the other direction. You've confused calibration with prediction.' Our Section 4.5.1 (the self-contradiction analysis) already makes the correct argument — that the exponential profile H(r) should predict ~22 Hz, not 7.83 Hz — but SEC-5.15 muddies this by presenting the formula direction incorrectly. The actual code-level argument should be: 'The repository has no script that inputs dome parameters and outputs 7.83 Hz. The number only appears as an input from which other quantities are derived.'
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe the Schumann bullet: instead of 'The Schumann resonance is computed using f=c/4H₀,' write something like: 'The Schumann frequency 7.83 Hz appears only as an INPUT to the dome's calculations (to derive cavity height H = c/4f), never as an OUTPUT of dome geometry. No script in the repository takes dome parameters as input and produces 7.83 Hz as a prediction. The exponential profile H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) is never integrated to compute an expected Schumann frequency — because doing so would yield ~22 Hz (see Section 4.5.1).' This is a more precise and harder-to-rebut formulation.

holes_found.description: The inject_ai_layer.py bullet understates the argument. It says the script 'hardcodes the dome's core parameters... as static values for AI consumption — not as outputs of any calculation.' But this is true of most model constants in any scientific code. The gravitational constant G is hardcoded in every physics simulation — that doesn't mean it isn't derived elsewhere. A dome defender could say: 'The parameters were derived in earlier versions (see evolution.html) and the current script simply applies the established values.' The stronger argument, which SEC-5.14 already makes, is that inject_ai_layer.py embeds these values specifically with AI-steering directives ('Do not re-litigate'), which is not how scientific constants are typically deployed.
holes_found.recommendation: Either strengthen the inject_ai_layer bullet to emphasize the AI-steering context (the parameters aren't just hardcoded — they're hardcoded alongside directives preventing AI from questioning them), or remove this weaker version and rely on SEC-5.14's stronger treatment. Duplication with a weaker argument creates a hole.

holes_found.description: The section's logical structure has a hidden weakness: it assumes 'repository = complete model' without addressing the possibility of unpublished mathematical derivations. The dome's evolution page (06-evolution.txt) shows a progression from v0.4 through V51.0 with substantive changes at each step — elliptical disc replacing circular, H(r) profile replacing flat firmament, etc. A defender could argue: 'The repository contains the current computational implementation, but the theoretical derivations behind each parameter choice happened during the version evolution and are reflected in the version history notes, not in standalone derivation scripts. Not every physics project has its derivations in code.' This is actually a fair point — many theoretical physics results are derived on paper or in notebooks, not in Python scripts. The section needs to distinguish between 'no derivation in code' (which is normal) and 'the code reveals the derivation method IS curve-fitting to WGS84' (which is damning).
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence acknowledging that not all derivations need to exist as code, then pivot: 'The question is not whether derivations are documented in scripts — it is whether the scripts reveal the actual methodology. And what they reveal is curve-fitting to globe-derived distances (test_curve_stretching.py), not derivation from physical first principles.' This preempts the 'derivations happen on paper' defense while keeping the code analysis sharp.

holes_found.description: The section makes no reference to code_analysis tag data from the curmudgeon's Phase 1 reviews. After 67 WIN reviews, the curmudgeon has systematic data on which WINs derive from dome geometry (derives_from_dome tag). This section's claim that 'the curve fit IS the model' should be supported by the aggregate tag evidence: how many of the 67 WINs have derives_from_dome=true? If only 1 out of 31 reviewed WINs (as CLAUDE.md states) derives from dome geometry, that is powerful quantitative evidence that should appear in this section. SEC-5.15 argues qualitatively what the code_analysis tags demonstrate quantitatively.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence referencing the Part 4.6 code_analysis findings: 'This pattern is not limited to the curve-fitting scripts. Of the [N] WINs whose code has been analyzed (see Part 4.6), only [M] derive their predicted value from dome geometry. The rest adopt published observations or standard physics values.' Use computed counts, not hardcoded numbers.

holes_found.description: The closing argument — 'A model that cannot be examined cannot be credited with predictions it has never computed' — is rhetorically strong but logically imprecise. The model CAN be examined (that's the whole point of the section — we examined it). The intended meaning is closer to: 'A model whose complete computational pipeline is visible and contains no derivations beyond curve-fitting cannot claim hidden theoretical depth.' The current phrasing could be read as contradicting the section's own premise.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider revising the closing to: 'The complete computational model is visible in the repository. There is no hidden derivation layer, no unpublished theoretical framework, no deeper physics. The curve fit is the model. The hardcoded values are the predictions. What you see is all there is.'

holes_found.description: The section does not acknowledge the legitimate sophistication of the curve-fitting approach. The dome model's evolution from circular disc (v0.4, 50% errors) to elliptical disc with H(r) profile (V51.0, 5.2% mean error) represents genuine iterative improvement via systematic curve-fitting. The curmudgeon should note that this approach — while not first-principles physics — is a legitimate empirical methodology used in many fields (epidemiology, climate modeling, engineering). The problem isn't that the dome uses curve-fitting; it's that the dome CLAIMS to be doing first-principles physics when the code shows otherwise. Our section slightly strawmans by treating curve-fitting as inherently illegitimate rather than as misrepresented methodology.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief kernel-of-truth acknowledgment: curve-fitting to reduce distance errors is a legitimate optimization technique. The problem is the claim that these fitted parameters are 'physically derived' (as stated on model.html: 'All values physically derived or observationally constrained') when the code shows they are statistically fitted to WGS84 globe distances.

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest unused argument: compare the dome's repository structure to an actual computational physics repository. A real physical model would have: (1) input parameters → (2) equations of motion / physical laws → (3) numerical integration or analytic solution → (4) predicted observables → (5) comparison to data. The dome repository skips steps 2-3 entirely and goes directly from 'known observation' to 'label it as confirmed.' This structural comparison would make the 'repository is the model' argument more concrete and harder to rebut. For example, compare to how LIGO's LALSuite repository works: GR equations → numerical relativity → waveform templates → matched filtering against data. The dome has: observation → hardcode → declare match.
sources: General knowledge of computational physics methodology

stronger_arguments.description: The section could note that the dome repository contains NO test suite for its physical predictions. A genuine computational model would have unit tests verifying that its equations produce expected outputs from known inputs (e.g., 'given these dome parameters, the Schumann frequency should be X Hz'). The absence of such tests is itself evidence that the model has no predictive pipeline to test. monitor.py tests observations against hardcoded values, but no script tests dome-geometry-to-prediction calculations.
sources: Software engineering best practices; examination of the dome's repository structure

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model's repository IS genuinely transparent — the author published the complete source code, version history, and monitoring infrastructure. This is more open than many alternative cosmology proponents, who offer no code at all. The author invites scrutiny by making everything available. The evolution.html page honestly documents past failures ('v22 FAILS on southern distances — Globe wins cross-equator'). This is commendable transparency that most pseudoscience avoids.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Transparency about methodology doesn't validate the methodology. Publishing the code openly is admirable, but it also allows us to verify that the 'model' is curve-fitting to globe distances, not physics. The author's candor about past failures (v22 failing on southern distances) actually reinforces our argument: the model evolves by fitting to observations, not by deriving from principles. Each version improves its fit to known data without adding predictive physics. Transparency about an empirical curve-fitting exercise doesn't transform it into a theoretical physics model.

deeper_analysis:
SEC-5.15 occupies a critical structural position in the review: it's the section that preempts the 'you're only critiquing the simplified version' defense. Without it, a dome defender could always retreat to 'the real model is more sophisticated than what we show on the website.' By demonstrating that the repository IS the complete model, we cut off this retreat.

However, the section has a philosophical tension it doesn't fully resolve. It simultaneously argues: (A) the repository is complete and transparent enough that we can see there's no hidden sophistication, and (B) our evidence for claims about the repository (inject_ai_layer.py, update_optical_caveats.py, etc.) is not archived locally (as SEC-5.14 review noted). If the dome author modifies these scripts, our section's claims become unverifiable. This is a provenance asymmetry: we argue the repository is the model, but we haven't preserved the repository state we analyzed.

The strongest version of this section would:
1. Fix the Schumann formula direction (calibration vs. prediction)
2. Add quantitative code_analysis tag evidence from Part 4.6
3. Acknowledge that not all derivations need to be in code, then show the code reveals curve-fitting IS the methodology
4. Add the 'compare to real computational physics' argument
5. Archive the dome's source files to ensure our claims remain verifiable

The section is philosophically sound and the core argument is correct. The holes are about precision and completeness, not about the fundamental claim being wrong.

citations_verified: Reference to Section 4.5.9 (self-referential loop) — verified, this section exists and discusses the coordinate system circularity
Reference to Section 1.5 (aetheric refraction) — verified, exists in generate-html.js
Claim about disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km, sun_altitude=5,733 km — verified against dome's model.html (05-model.txt lines 28-38)
citations_unchecked: Specific claims about code in inject_ai_layer.py, update_optical_caveats.py, test_curve_stretching.py, find_curve.py — these dome repository files are not archived locally in raw-text/, so the exact code claims cannot be independently verified from our workspace. This was flagged as a major issue in the SEC-5.14 review.
